name: Tomatoes
notes: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost

preservation.notes: Whole, crushed, or as sauce; add lemon juice for safe acidity
Blanch and peel, or freeze whole for later sauce-making
Sun-dried or dehydrator; store in airtight container or oil

description: The tomato is the fruit of the tomato plant, a member of the Nightshade family (Solanaceae). The fruit grows on a small compact bush.

name: Bell Peppers

preservation.notes: Dice or slice; flash freeze on trays then bag
Pickle strips in vinegar brine with garlic
Dehydrate and grind into pepper flakes or powder

description: The bell pepper is a cultivar group of the species Capsicum annuum. Bell pepper cultivars produce fruits in colors including red, yellow, orange, green, brown, white, and purple. The fruit is often mildly sweet, because this specific cultivar does not produce capsaicin, the chemical responsible for other peppers' spiciness.

name: Hot Peppers
notes: Harvest at full color for maximum heat

preservation.notes: String dry whole peppers or use dehydrator; grind into powder
Pickle whole or sliced in vinegar brine
Freeze whole or diced; no blanching needed
Classic dill pickles or bread-and-butter; water-bath can
Lacto-fermented pickles in salt brine; keep refrigerated

name: Lettuce
notes: Direct sow 2 weeks before last frost

preservation.notes: Dehydrate leaves for powder to add to smoothies or soups
Freeze for smoothies only; texture not suitable for salads after thawing

description: Lettuce is a cool weather crop and high temperatures will impede germination and/or cause the plant to bolt (go to seed quickly). Some hybrid cultivars have been bred to be more heat-resistant.

name: Bush Beans
notes: Wait for soil to warm to 60°F
Do not soak seeds before planting

preservation.notes: Pressure can whole or cut green beans
Blanch 3 minutes, ice bath, then freeze in bags
Let pods mature on vine for dry shell beans

name: Pole Beans
notes: Longer harvest period than bush beans

preservation.notes: Pressure can whole or cut green beans
Blanch 3 minutes, ice bath, then freeze in bags
Let pods dry on vine for shell beans; store in jars

name: Summer Squash
notes: Pick when small for best flavor

preservation.notes: Shred for zucchini bread or slice and blanch before freezing
Slice thin for zucchini chips; rehydrate for soups
Best canned as relish or pickled; not safe to can plain
Cure 2 weeks in sun then store at 50-55°F; butternut lasts longest
Pressure can cubed (not mashed) for long-term storage
Cook and puree before freezing; great for soups and pies

name: Carrots
notes: Direct sow 3 weeks before last frost

preservation.notes: Pack in damp sand at 32-40°F; trim tops first
Blanch 3 minutes, ice bath, dice or slice, then freeze
Pressure can sliced or diced carrots

description: The carrot is a root vegetable. It is usually orange in color, but some cultivars are purple, black, red, white, and yellow. The most commonly eaten part of the plant is the taproot, but the greens are sometimes eaten as well. The leaves appear first, and the taproot grows more slowly beneath the soil. Fast-growing cultivars mature within three months of sowing the seed. Slower-maturing cultivars are harvested four months after sowing.

name: Radishes
notes: Harvest when small for best flavor

preservation.notes: Quick pickle sliced radishes in rice vinegar
Lacto-ferment with salt; traditional Korean kkakdugi

description: Radishes are fast-growing cool season root vegetables in the Brassica family. Their taproots come in variety of shapes, sizes, and colors and have a sharp, spicy taste. They are usually eaten raw and added to salads. Radishes need cool weather and moist soil - they do not do well in hot, dry environments. More growing information is available in individual species entries.

name: Spinach

preservation.notes: Blanch briefly, squeeze dry, freeze in portions for smoothies or cooking
Dehydrate leaves and crumble into powder for soups and smoothies

description: Spinach is an annual plant whose deep green leaves are eaten as a vegetable. It grows best in cooler weather. It can be eaten raw or cooked.

name: Kale

preservation.notes: Blanch, chop, and freeze in portions; great for smoothies and soups
Make kale chips or powder for nutrient-dense seasoning

description: Kale is a cultivar of the species Brassica oleracea. It has green or purple leaves that branch off from one to multiple upright stems and do not form a head like cabbage. The plant is usually grown as an annual, but if grown as a perennial, it will form seeds in the second year. Current popular varieties include Curly kale, Italian kale, and Red Russian kale (green leaves with pale purple stems). It can be grown as baby salad greens or for bunching adult leaves. Leaves are sweeter after a frost and delicious eaten raw, added to salads, sautéed, or added to stews and casseroles.

name: Broccoli

preservation.notes: Cut into florets, blanch 3 minutes, ice bath, freeze on trays
Dehydrate florets for soups and casseroles
Pickle florets in seasoned vinegar brine

description: Broccoli has large flower heads known as "crowns" that are green to blue-green in color, grouped tightly together atop a thick stem, and surrounded by leaves. Broccoli resembles cauliflower, a different cultivar in its species. It thrives in cool weather.

preservation.notes: Cure in dry area 2 weeks then store at 35-50°F; storage varieties last longest
Slice thin and dehydrate; grind into onion powder
Pickle pearl onions or sliced red onions in vinegar

name: Garlic
notes: Plant in FALL (Sept-Oct) for summer harvest
Cold period required for bulb formation
Harvest when 50% of leaves are brown

preservation.notes: Cure 2 weeks then store at 60-65°F; softneck stores longer than hardneck
Slice and dehydrate; grind into garlic powder
Store peeled cloves in oil in refrigerator only; botulism risk at room temp

description: The garlic plant has a bulbous root separated into cloves and a tall stalk with branching leaves. The plant has two main subspecies: hard and soft neck. The choice between which subspecies to grow depends on latitude and at what point in the growing season you will be planting the crop: garlic can be day-length sensitive. Hard-neck garlic is usually grown in cooler climates and creates larger cloves. Soft-neck garlic is grown in warmer climates and produces smaller, tightly-packed cloves. Be sure to trim garlic scapes before they flower - this will focus the plant's energy into bulb growth, resulting in larger cloves and bulbs. Scapes have a fresh, light garlic flavor. They are delicious raw or cooked, and make a great pesto.

name: Potatoes
notes: Plant seed potatoes 2 weeks after last frost
Hill soil around plants as they grow
Harvest new potatoes early, storage potatoes late

preservation.notes: Cure 2 weeks at 60°F then store at 40°F in dark; check for sprouts
Blanch cubes or mash before freezing; do not freeze raw
Pressure can cubed potatoes; great for quick meals

description: Potatoes are starchy root vegetables in the Solanaceae, or Nightshade, family, which also includes tomatoes, eggplants, and peppers. They originated in South America, and spread to become a worldwide staple. The leaves and fruit are usually poisonous and the stem tuber is the only edible part once it is cooked. The potato can be cooked in many ways, brewed into alcohol, and also used as the basis for creating bioplastics. More growing information is available in individual species entries.

name: Corn
notes: Plant multiple varieties with different maturity dates

preservation.notes: Blanch ears 4 minutes, cut kernels, freeze in bags
Pressure can kernels or cream-style corn
Dry mature kernels for grinding into cornmeal or popcorn

description: Corn is a large grain plant, or tall grass, first domesticated about 10,000 years ago by indigenous peoples in Southern Mexico. The leafy stalk produces ears after pollination. Depending on the variety, the corn can be eaten fresh, or dried and ground into cornmeal.

preservation.notes: Blanch shelled peas 2 minutes, ice bath, freeze flat on trays
Pressure can shelled peas; snap peas better frozen
Let pods dry on vine for split pea soup stock

name: Beets
notes: Direct sow 2 weeks before last frost

preservation.notes: Trim tops to 1 inch; store in damp sand at 32-40°F
Classic pickled beets in vinegar and spice brine; water-bath can
Pressure can whole small beets or sliced

description: Typically grown for its round or cylindrical taproot, the leaves of the beet are also edible. Leaves resemble Swiss chard on a smaller scale and are good sautéed. The taproot, or beet, ranges in color from deep red to gold, orange, or white and red striped. The beet has a delicious, earthy flavor with a touch of sweetness to it when roasted.

name: Swiss Chard
notes: Direct sow 1 week before last frost

preservation.notes: Blanch leaves and stems separately; freeze in portions
Dehydrate leaves for powder; stems do not dehydrate well

description: Swiss Chard is a leafy green vegetable in the Beet family. The thick stems come in a variety of colors including white, pink, red, yellow, orange, and pale green. It has large, crinkly leaves with distinctive veins. The leaves and stems are edible and high in nutrients. Swiss chard can be eaten raw or sautéed, baked, or steamed.

name: Eggplant
notes: Very sensitive to cold; do not transplant until nights are consistently above 60F

preservation.notes: Roast or blanch slices before freezing
Dehydrate slices for eggplant jerky

description: Eggplants commonly are egg-shaped with glossy black skin, but can come in a variety of other shapes and colors. They can be white, yellow, and pale to deep purple. Some are as small as goose eggs. The 'Rosa Bianca' cultivar is squat and round, while Asian cultivars can be long and thin. Eggplant stems are often spiny and their flowers range from white to purple. Their flesh is generally white with a meaty texture and small seeds in the center. They are delicious grilled, roasted, in soups and stews, and breaded and fried.

name: Kohlrabi
notes: Harvest when the swollen stem is 2-3 inches in diameter; becomes woody if left too long

preservation.notes: Remove leaves and store stem in crisper
Blanch cubes and freeze

description: Kohlrabi is a low, stout cultivar of cabbage that has a swollen, almost spherical stem with leaves sprouting off the top. The stem can be light green, white, or purple. Both the stem and leaves are edible. The stem's taste and texture is similar to a broccoli stem or cabbage heart, but milder and sweeter. Younger stems can be as crisp and juicy as apples, but much less sweet. Older stems tend to be woody. Like other members of the Brassica family, kohlrabi prefers mild, cool weather.

name: Celery
notes: Requires extremely consistent moisture; drying out causes bitter, stringy stalks

preservation.notes: Chop and freeze for soups (loses crispness)
Dry leaves and stalks for celery powder

description: The celery plant has long fibrous stalks that taper into leaves. The stalks and leaves can both be eaten. Celery seed is also used as a spice. Celery seed extracts are used in medicines.

name: Parsnip
notes: Leave in the ground until after the first hard frost to convert starches to sugars
Seed viability drops rapidly; always use fresh seed

preservation.notes: Store in damp sand
Blanch chunks and freeze

description: Parsnips are a root vegetable closely related to carrots and celery. They look like thick white carrots with larger, slightly rough foliage. Parsnips taste similar to carrots, except that they are sweeter, especially when cooked. Like carrots, they can be broiled, pureed, roasted, steamed, and baked into cakes. They become even sweeter in flavor after winter frosts. Before cane sugar arrived in Europe, they were used as a sweetener. The stems and foliage of the parsnip can cause a skin rash - wear long sleeves, pants, and gloves when weeding and harvesting.

name: Asparagus
notes: Do not harvest at all the first year; harvest lightly the second year

preservation.notes: Blanch quickly and freeze
Pressure can spears
Pickle spears in a vinegar brine

description: Perennial spring vegetable often sown from crowns. The vegetable has a mild flavor with earthy undertones. When mature and reproducing, the plant creates tall, stout stems with feathery foliage and small red berries.

name: Cauliflower
notes: To keep the head white, tie outer leaves over the developing curd ('blanching')

preservation.notes: Blanch florets and freeze
Pickle florets in vinegar brine

description: Cauliflower is a vegetable in the Brassicaceae family. The solid, firm head resembles that of broccoli and is usually white, but can also be yellow, purple, or green in color. Like broccoli, it sits atop a stalk. The head is wrapped in thick leaves that begin to open when the plant is ready for harvest. All cauliflower does best in cool weather.

name: Cabbage
notes: Harvest before heads split; splitting is triggered by sudden rain after dry period
Rotate with non-brassica crops each year to prevent clubroot buildup
Savoy types are the most frost-tolerant — can handle light freezes in the field

preservation.notes: Store whole heads at 32-40°F with high humidity; do not wash before storage
Salt-ferment shredded cabbage for sauerkraut; no canning equipment needed
Blanch wedges or shredded cabbage before freezing

description: Cabbage is a member of the Brassica family and related to kale, broccoli, brussels sprouts, and cauliflower. It's dense, layered heads grow on stalks and are surrounded by looser outer leaves. It's leaves can be green, white, or purple in color, and smooth or crinkly in texture. Depending on the variety, the head can be round, oblong, or flat. Cabbage prefers cooler temperatures and is best planted in the spring or fall.
